content.text: LangSmith evaluation is a systematic way to measure how well an LLM application performs on a curated set of examples. You define a dataset of inputs and reference outputs, run your app against every example, and score each result with one or more evaluator functions. The scores are recorded as an experiment in LangSmith, letting you compare prompt versions, models, and retrieval strategies quantitatively over time.

attrs.alt: LangSmith evaluation pipeline: dataset feeds into app under test, evaluators score each run, results recorded in LangSmith UI for comparison

content.text: A dataset is a named collection of input/output pairs. You can create one programmatically with the Python SDK or by clicking 'Add to dataset' on any trace in the LangSmith UI. Datasets are reusable — the same dataset can be used across many evaluation runs as your application evolves.

from langsmith import Client

client = Client()

# Create the dataset (idempotent helper pattern)
DATASET_NAME = "rag-qa-v1"
if not client.has_dataset(dataset_name=DATASET_NAME):
    ds = client.create_dataset(
        DATASET_NAME,
        description="Factual QA examples for the RAG pipeline",
    )
    client.create_examples(
        inputs=[
            {"question": "What year was Python created?"},
            {"question": "Who invented the transformer architecture?"},
            {"question": "What does RAG stand for?"},
        ],
        outputs=[
            {"answer": "1991"},
            {"answer": "Vaswani et al. (Google Brain), 2017"},
            {"answer": "Retrieval-Augmented Generation"},
        ],
        dataset_id=ds.id,
    )

# Add examples from production traces (batch)
runs = list(client.list_runs(
    project_name="production",
    run_type="chain",
    limit=20,
))
client.create_examples(
    inputs=[r.inputs for r in runs],
    outputs=[r.outputs for r in runs],
    dataset_name=DATASET_NAME,
)
print(f"Dataset '{DATASET_NAME}' ready")
An evaluator is a Python function that receives a
). It returns a dict with at least a
Pass your app function, the dataset name, and a list of evaluators to
. LangSmith calls your function once per dataset example, applies each evaluator to the result, stores every scored run as an experiment, and returns an
object you can convert to a pandas DataFrame.

from langsmith.evaluation import evaluate
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage

# The function under test — must accept a dict matching dataset inputs
def run_rag(inputs: dict) -> dict:
    question = inputs["question"]
    result = app.invoke({"messages": [HumanMessage(content=question)]})
    return {"answer": result["messages"][-1].content}

results = evaluate(
    run_rag,
    data=DATASET_NAME,
    evaluators=[exact_match, contains_answer, brevity],
    experiment_prefix="gpt-4o-mini-v1",   # shown in the UI
    num_repetitions=1,                      # run each example once
    max_concurrency=4,                      # parallel calls
)

df = results.to_pandas()
print(df[["inputs.question", "outputs.answer", "feedback.exact_match",
          "feedback.contains_answer", "feedback.brevity"]].to_string())
print(f"\nMean exact_match: {df['feedback.exact_match'].mean():.2f}")

LangSmith appends a timestamp to your prefix to make each run unique. The experiment appears in the LangSmith UI under the dataset's Experiments tab. Multiple experiments on the same dataset are side-by-side comparable.
When correctness cannot be determined by string matching — for open-ended generation, code quality, or reasoning — use a second LLM to judge the output. LangSmith ships ready-made LLM evaluators via

After running two or more experiments on the same dataset, open the dataset's Experiments tab in the LangSmith UI. The comparison view shows each metric as a column and each experiment as a row, with the score distribution per example. Click any cell to see the actual run that produced that score.
Green / red highlighting: LangSmith colours cells relative to the baseline experiment you select.
Filter failures: click 'score = 0' in any evaluator column to list only the failed examples — the fastest path to understanding where the model breaks.
Share link: every experiment has a shareable URL for async review with teammates.
CSV export: download the scored table for offline analysis.

from langsmith import Client

client = Client()

# List all experiments on the dataset
experiments = list(client.list_projects(reference_dataset_name=DATASET_NAME))

for exp in experiments:
    runs = list(client.list_runs(project_name=exp.name))
    feedback = client.list_feedback(
        run_ids=[str(r.id) for r in runs],
        feedback_key="exact_match",
    )
    scores = [fb.score for fb in feedback if fb.score is not None]
    avg = sum(scores) / len(scores) if scores else 0.0
    print(f"{exp.name:50s}  exact_match={avg:.3f}  n={len(scores)}")

For generation tasks where you want to compare two model outputs directly rather than scoring each against a fixed reference, use pairwise evaluation. An LLM judge receives both outputs and picks the better one.
CI Integration — Running Evaluations on Every PR
Automate evaluation in your CI pipeline to catch regressions before they reach production. Store the expected minimum score as a threshold and fail the CI job if the evaluation drops below it.

# eval_ci.py — run with: python eval_ci.py
import sys
from langsmith.evaluation import evaluate

THRESHOLD = 0.80   # fail CI if mean exact_match < 80%

results = evaluate(
    run_rag,
    data=DATASET_NAME,
    evaluators=[exact_match],
    experiment_prefix="ci-pr",
)
df = results.to_pandas()
mean_score = df["feedback.exact_match"].mean()
print(f"exact_match mean: {mean_score:.3f} (threshold: {THRESHOLD})")

if mean_score < THRESHOLD:
    print("FAIL: score below threshold")
    sys.exit(1)
print("PASS")

# .github/workflows/eval.yml
name: LangSmith Evaluation
on: [pull_request]
jobs:
  eval:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.11" }
      - run: pip install langsmith langchain-openai
      - run: python eval_ci.py
        env:
          LANGCHAIN_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.LANGCHAIN_API_KEY }}
          OPENAI_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.OPENAI_API_KEY }}
          LANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2: "true"
          LANGCHAIN_PROJECT: "ci-evals"
Business logic, structured output parsing, schema validation
A/B comparison of two model variants
Capturing Online Feedback as Evaluation Data

Production feedback from real users is valuable evaluation data. When a user rates a response, record it as a LangSmith feedback score on the corresponding run. Over time, accumulated ratings form a signal you can use to build a curated dataset of high-confidence positive and negative examples for offline evaluation.

from langsmith import Client
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel

client = Client()
api = FastAPI()

class FeedbackRequest(BaseModel):
    run_id: str
    rating: int      # 1 = thumbs-up, 0 = thumbs-down
    comment: str = ""

@api.post("/feedback")
async def record_feedback(req: FeedbackRequest):
    client.create_feedback(
        run_id=req.run_id,
        key="user_rating",
        score=req.rating,
        comment=req.comment,
    )
    return {"ok": True}

# Later: pull thumbs-down runs and add them as negative examples to a dataset
negative_runs = list(client.list_runs(
    project_name="production",
    filter='and(eq(feedback_key, "user_rating"), eq(feedback_score, 0))',
    limit=50,
))
client.create_examples(
    inputs=[r.inputs for r in negative_runs],
    outputs=[r.outputs for r in negative_runs],
    dataset_name="failure-cases-v1",
)
print(f"Added {len(negative_runs)} failure cases to dataset")

thumbs-down examples collected from production make the best evaluation dataset because they represent real failure modes, not synthetic edge cases. Curate them into a dataset, run offline evaluation after each model/prompt change, and track whether the failure rate decreases.
Evaluator functions are pure Python — test them with standard pytest without hitting the LangSmith API. Mock the
A LangSmith dataset is a named collection of input/output examples created with client.create_dataset() + client.create_examples().
evaluate(fn, data=dataset_name, evaluators=[...]) calls fn on every example, scores each result, and records the experiment.
Evaluators are Python functions with signature (run, example) -> {key, score} — return scores between 0 and 1 (or any numeric range).
LLM-as-judge evaluators use a second model to score open-ended outputs; LangChainStringEvaluator('criteria', ...) provides a ready-made version.
Pairwise evaluation with evaluate_comparative() lets a judge pick the better answer between two experiment variants directly.
Automate evaluation in CI by running evaluate() in a script that exits non-zero when mean score falls below a threshold.
The Experiments tab in the LangSmith UI shows side-by-side metric comparisons with drill-down into individual failed examples.
